coredata.dc:description: In order to fulfill people's requirements for food quality and safety, it is a promising strategy to develop intelligent biodegradable food packaging materials. Herein, honeysuckle extracts/attapulgite/chitosan composite films containing natural carbon dots were fabricated for intelligent food packaging. Different characterization techniques were employed to study the obtained composite films, while the physicochemical properties, optical properties, antibacterial and antioxidant activities of composite films were determined. The obtained composite films presented good mechanical, antibacterial and antioxidant properties, and the antibacterial ratios of composite films against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus were 99.27 ± 0.18 % and 98.85 ± 0.65 %, respectively. When the added amount of honeysuckle extracts/attapulgite nanocomposites was 4.76 %, the tensile strength and elongation at break of composite films reached 24.9 ± 2.35 MPa and 64.8 ± 2.11 %, respectively, which were obviously higher than that of pure chitosan film. Furthermore, the composite films exhibited excellent UV shielding and blue fluorescence properties, as well as pH-sensitivity due to the presence of caffeoquinic acid-based natural carbon dots derived from honeysuckle extract. Therefore, the composite films indicated a potential application for intelligent food packaging.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional honeysuckle extract/attapulgite/chitosan composite films containing natural carbon dots for intelligent food packaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In order to fulfill people's requirements for food quality and safety, it is a promising strategy to develop intelligent biodegradable food packaging materials. Herein, honeysuckle extracts/attapulgite/chitosan composite films containing natural carbon dots were fabricated for intelligent food packaging. Different characterization techniques were employed to study the obtained composite films, while the physicochemical properties, optical properties, antibacterial and antioxidant activities of composite films were determined. The obtained composite films presented good mechanical, antibacterial and antioxidant properties, and the antibacterial ratios of composite films against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus were 99.27 ± 0.18 % and 98.85 ± 0.65 %, respectively. When the added amount of honeysuckle extracts/attapulgite nanocomposites was 4.76 %, the tensile strength and elongation at break of composite films reached 24.9 ± 2.35 MPa and 64.8 ± 2.11 %, respectively, which were obviously higher than that of pure chitosan film. Furthermore, the composite films exhibited excellent UV shielding and blue fluorescence properties, as well as pH-sensitivity due to the presence of caffeoquinic acid-based natural carbon dots derived from honeysuckle extract. Therefore, the composite films indicated a potential application for intelligent food packaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In order to solve food safety and food waste problems caused by food spoilage, a variety of packaging materials were developed for food preservation to extend shelf life. Therefore, different synthetic polymers of petrochemical origin, such as polypropylene, polyethylene, polystyrene and polyethylene terephthalate, were designed as disposable food packaging materials [1]. However, the extensive use of petroleum-based polymer packaging materials resulted in serious environmental problems, and the biodegradable packaging materials provided a sustainable strategy for controlling food safety and reducing packaging waste due to their environmental friendliness and natural availability. Thus, multi-functional biodegradable food packaging has become one of the research hotspots in the food industry [2]. For example, an edible pH-sensitive film combined with electrochemical writing was developed using gelatin, gellan gum, and carrot anthocyanin extracts to indicate the quality of milk and fish [3], while active chitosan/polyvinyl alcohol films with anthocyanins as time-temperature indicators were prepared based on chitosan (CS), polyvinyl alcohol, and natural extracts to indicate the change of the food quality [4].

Generally, the biodegradable food packaging films mainly consisted of polymeric matrix and active nanofiller. Among various natural polymers for food packaging films, CS (b-(1,4)-2-amino-2-deoxy-d-glucose) is the deacetylation product of chitin, which has the advantages of innocuity, biocompatibility, biodegradability, anti-inflammatory and antibacterial [5]. It has been confirmed that incorporation of nano-fillers into the natural polymer matrix can improve the mechanical properties and vapor permeability of pure polymer films, and endow with new functions derived from active components [6]. Attapulgite (APT) is a natural 2:1 layered-chain hydrous magnesium‑aluminum clay mineral with unique one-dimensional rod-like morphology, zeolite-like channels and abundant surface hydroxyl groups [6,7]. Zhang et al. [8] evaluated the potential of silicate nanorods derived from natural attapulgite as nanofillers to improve the mechanical, thermal stability, and aging resistance of CS/polyvinyl pyrrolidone blending films. However, it is difficult to provide new functions for the composite films only depending on pure APT, such as antibacterial and antioxidant activity, and it is indispensable to loading of bioactive matters based on the carrier and adsorption functions of APT.

Carbon dots (CDs) are new zero-dimensional fluorescent carbon nanomaterials with a size of 10 nm [9]. It has attracted widespread attention due to its excellent properties such as low toxicity, good biocompatibility, excellent antioxidant and antibacterial activity [10]. Due to the various advantages of natural products, different methods were developed for the preparation of natural product-derived carbon dots (NCDs), include hydrothermal treatment, microwave treatment, carbonization, extraction and chemical oxidation [11]. Compared with the common chemical synthesis, some natural products containing NCDs could be extracted directly without synthesis process [12]. For example, Dinç extracted NCDs with strong blue fluorescence from a sugar beet molasses to detect riboflavin and tetracycline [13]. What's more, the introduction of NCDs into the films is an effective strategy to provide the fluorescence properties for the films, and simultaneously enhance the antibacterial and antioxidant activities of pure CS films, and thus active packaging composite films were successfully prepared for fresh pork preservation by incorporating fluorescent NCDs derived from natural plant turmeric into CS matrix [14].

Honeysuckle is widely distributed in East Asia and used in Chinese herbal medicine with anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, antipyretic, hepatoprotective and choleretic effects [15]. It was reported that the major chemical components in ethanol extracts of honeysuckle mainly included organic acids, flavonoids, terpenes, triterpene saponins, etc. [16]. The organic acids in honeysuckle extract mainly consist of chlorogenic acid and isochlorogenic acid. Zhang et al. [17] dispersed chlorogenic acid in methanol solution, and then the carbon dots with photoluminescence were prepared by stirring and storing at room temperature. Therefore, novel honeysuckle extracts/APT/CS composite films (HEAC) containing natural carbon dots with excellent blue fluorescence were fabricated for intelligent food packaging, in which honeysuckle extracts/APT nanocomposite (HEA) was facilely prepared via a cleaner mechanical grinding (Fig. 1 ). The physical properties, antioxidant and antibacterial activity of the as-prepared composite films were investigated. In addition, the UV shielding properties, environmental sensitivity toward pH, and fruit preservation application were also studied. It is expected to develop intelligent food packaging composite films combining with natural clay minerals and plant extract containing carbon dots.

APT was purchased from Xuyi Oubaite Clay Materials Co., Ltd. (Jiangsu, China). CS with deacetylation degree of 85 % and average molecular weight of 800 KDa was obtained from Xingcheng Biological Products Factory Co., Ltd. (Jiangsu, China). Honeysuckle extracts was gained from Xi'an Xiaocao Botanical Development Co., Ltd. (Shanxi, China), in which the content of chlorogenic acid was 25 %. Glycerol (≥99.0 %), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, ≥99.5 %), analytical-grade ethanol (≥99.7 %) and glacial acetic acid (≥99.5 %) were acquired from Li’anlong Bohua Pharmaceutical Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB, ≥99.0 %) and salicylic acid (≥99.5 %) were purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Co., Ltd. (Sichuan, China). 2,2-Diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH, ≥97.0 %) and 1,3-Diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF, ≥97.0 %) were obtained from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). FeSO4·7H2O (≥99.0 %) and Mg(NO3)2·6H2O (≥99.0 %) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Works (Beijing, China). Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, ≥30 %) was obtained from Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd. (Sichuan, China). Escherichia coli (E. coli, ATCC 25922) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, ATCC 25923) were acquired from Laboratory Medicine Center, Lanzhou University Second Hospital. The bacterial strains were cultured and diluted with nutrient agar medium and LB broth, respectively, which were obtained from Qingdao Hope Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Shandong, China).

HEA nanocomposites were prepared by incorporating honeysuckle extracts into CTAB modified APT under a mechanical milling method. First, 0.25 g of CTAB was dissolved in 50 mL of ultrapure water, and 5.0 g of APT was added and stirred magnetically (300 rpm) for 6 h at room temperature. Then, the mixture was centrifuged (4000 rpm) and washed for 3 times with ultrapure water. The products were dried for 2 h in an oven at 60 °C. Next, 5 g of CTAB modified APT and 0.56 g of honeysuckle extract were mixed and ground in a mortar for 30 min at room temperature, and then the products were dried for 2 h in an oven at 60 °C to obtain HEA nanocomposites.

First, HEA nanocomposites were dispersed in ultrapure water to prepare a suspension of 10 g/L. Second, 20.0 g of CS was dissolved in ultrapure water (970 mL), and 10 g of glacial acetic acid was added to dissolve CS. Next, the mixed solution was stirred mechanically (200 rpm) for 6 h at room temperature and obtained CS solution (2 wt%). Different volumes of the suspension containing HEA nanocomposites, ultrapure water, glycerol (1.6 g) and chitosan solution (50 mL) were mixed and stirred mechanically for 2 h at room temperature, the formulas of the samples were shown in Table S1, and then the mixed solution was poured into petri dishes, and kept for 72 h at room temperature to obtain HEAC composite films, which were marked as HEAC-1, HEAC-2, HEAC-3, HEAC-4 with the increase in the added amounts of HEA nanocomposites, respectively (Table S1).

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the samples were captured using a JSM-6701F scanning electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) at 15 kV. The fracture surface of the films was obtained by immersing them in liquid nitrogen and cryo-fractured manually, and all samples were coated with a thin layer of gold. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of the relevant samples were acquired on a JEM-1200EX/S transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained by a STA8000 FTIR spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA) in the 4000–400 cm−1 range. The ATR-FTIR spectra of the films were measured by a NEXUS 870 FTIR spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA) using the attenuated total reflectance (ATR) mode within the wavelength range of 4000–500 cm−1 at a resolution of 4 cm−1. The phase analysis of the films was analyzed via the X-ray diffraction (XRD) (X'Pert PRO, PAN analytical, λ = 1.54060 Å, U = 40 kV). The thermal behavior of the samples was carried out using an STA 8000/Frontier thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA, Perkin-Elmer, USA) under constant N2 flow (10.0 mL·min−1), while the photoluminescence and photoluminescence excitation spectra were recorded by a fluorescence spectrometer (Omni-λ300i, Zolix Instruments Co., Ltd., China) with an excitation source of 500 W Xe 900 lamp.

The thickness of HEAC composite films was measured by the electronic outside micrometer with the accuracy of 0.001 mm at different positions on each film. All measurements were repeated for three times and the results averaged (Table S2).

The water content (WC), swelling degree (SD), water solubility (WS) of the films were evaluated by the method of Kalaycıoğlu et al. [18]. Three parallel experiments were performed for each sample. The square films (2 cm × 2 cm) were weighed to the nearest ~0.0001 g (m1). Then, the films were dried at 70 °C for 24 h and weighed for their dry mass (m2). The dried films were soaked in 50 mL of ultrapure water for 24 h and weighed immediately (m3) after removed excess water with the filter paper. The films were re-dried at 70 °C for 24 h and weighed to obtain the final quality of the film (m4). The WC, SD and WS values of the films were calculated by Eqs. (1)–(3), respectively.

The surface wettability of the films was evaluated by measuring the water contact angle (WCA) with a Contact Angle System OCA20 (Dataphysics, Germany). At room temperature, the distilled water (5 μL) was released to the surface of the films, the contact angle was calculated automatically and photographed (2000 fps). Each group of experiments was repeated for five times and the results averaged.

Tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break (EB) of the films were measured by a New SANS universal material testing system (CMT4304, Shenzhen SANS Test Machine Co. Ltd., China) according to ASTM D882–02. The films were cut into rectangles shape (80 mm × 10 mm) and equilibrated in a desiccator with a fixed relative humidity (RH = 53 %) at room temperature for 72 h, which was controlled by the saturated aqueous solution of Mg(NO3)2. The sensor used for the test was 50 N, the initial grip distribution and crosshead speed were set at 40 mm and 10 mm/min, respectively. The TS and EB were measured at least five times and the results averaged.

A UV 765 spectrophotometer (Precision & Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd., China) was used to measure the light transmittance of the films (10 mm × 42 mm). The wavelength range was 200–800 nm, and plot data were recorded every 0.5 nm. Furthermore, the color values (L

The fluorescence emission spectra of the films were recorded at excitation wavelengths from 350 nm to 365 nm to determine the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths, and the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded. As for pH sensing, HEAC-3 composite film was selected to be immersed into different pH solutions of 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10 and 11 respectively.

The antioxidant activity was measured using the hydroxyl radicals (·OH) method according to Smirnoff and Cumbes [20]. Specifically, the reaction system contained 4.0 mL FeSO4 (9.0 mM), 4 mL salicylic acid in anhydrous ethanol (9.0 mM), 4.0 mL ultrapure water and 100 mg of the films. Then, 0.4 mL H2O2 (0.3 %) was added to start the reaction. The mixed solution was incubated at 37 °C for 30 min, and the absorbance was measured at 510 nm. No film samples in the same process served as the blank group while 0.4 mL ultrapure water instead of H2O2 was used as the control group. The ·OH scavenging activity was calculated by the following formula:
(5)
· OH

%
=
1 −
A 1
−
A 2
/
A 0
× 100 %
where A1 is the absorbance value of the samples (reaction solution with sample), A2 is the absorbance value of the blank group and A0 is the absorbance value of the control group (reaction solution with no sample).

The antioxidant activity was further evaluated using the salicylate spectrophotometric method with a minor modification [21]. Briefly, 100 mg of the films were immersed into 10 mL of 20 mg/L ethanol solution of DPPH. The mixture was incubated in the dark at 25 °C for 30 min. The absorbance was measured at 517 nm by a UV–vis spectrophotometer. The radical scavenging activity for DPPH was calculated form the following formula:
(6)

A 0

where A0 is the absorbance of the control (initial DPPH solution with no sample); At is the absorbance of the test sample (DPPH solution with sample).

1,3-Diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) was selected as a specific singlet oxygen quencher to investigate singlet oxygen photogeneration. The HEAC-3 composite films were attached to the inner wall of a cuvette containing 0.05 mmol/L solution of DPBF in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), and the decrease in the absorbance of DPBF was monitored at 410 nm, while no film was served as the control group.

The antibacterial activities of the samples were evaluated using the colony count method [22]. In this experiment, E. coli and S. aureus were chosen as the representative Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria, respectively. All the films (3 cm × 3 cm) were sterilized under an ultraviolet lamp for 30 min, and then added into 10 mL of the bacterial dispersions and mildly shaken in a shaker (HNY-200B, Tianjin, China) at 37 °C for 24 h. Afterward, bacterial dispersion was diluted to 105 times and spread over the surface of the nutrient agar media using an inoculating loop. The petri dishes were incubated in a carbon dioxide incubator (5 % CO2) at 37 °C for 24 h, and the surviving colonies in each disk were photographed and counted. The positive and negative control groups were set, and the antibacterial efficiency was calculated by Eq. (7) [23]:
(7)
Antibacterial effectiveness
%
=
A − B
/ A × 100 %
where A is the bacterial count for the control and B is the bacterial count for each sample.

The purchased fresh strawberries were picked on the same day, and the strawberries without disease and damage were selected for the experiments. First, the strawberries were soaked into 0.2 % NaClO aqueous solution for 5 min to remove surface stains, washed three times with ultrapure water to remove NaClO and dried at room temperature. Second, the pre-treated strawberries were randomly divided into six groups with three fruits each, and immersed into the pure CS solution or precursor solution containing HEA nanocomposites for 20 min. Next, the coated strawberries were placed on a perforated tray for 15 min to remove excess coating solution, while one group was untreated to be as control. Finally, all the strawberries were stored at room temperature (25 ± 2 °C), and the photographs of them were taken on different days. In order to indirectly confirm the consistency of the coating films and free-standing films prepared by dip coating and casting method, respectively, the pre-treated strawberries were also packaged with HEAC composite films and placed in a glass dish (three strawberries per group) to investigate the fruit preservation.

The statistical analysis of the experimental results was done using the SPSS23 software (SPSS INC., IBM, New York, USA). The model was proved through analysis of variance (ANOVA). The differences between the average values were evaluated by Duncan's test. The significance level was p ≤ 0.05.

In the FTIR spectrum of honeysuckle extract (Fig. 2a), the broad signal peak at 3404 cm−1 corresponded to -OH groups of the organic components in honeysuckle extract [24]. The peaks at 1596 cm−1 and 1517 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of aromatic ring, and the band at 1277 cm−1 was caused by C-O-C stretching vibrations [15]. In the case of APT, the peaks at 3619 and 3549 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of Mg/Al/Fe-OH, and the Si-O-Si stretching vibration, Si-O-Mg stretching vibration and the bending vibration of O-Si-O were observed at 1027 cm−1, 975 cm−1 and 472 cm−1, respectively. The CH antisymmetric stretching modes in alkanes of CTAB modified APT was observed at 2930 and 2855 cm−1, indicating that APT was successfully modified by CTAB [25]. After incorporation of honeysuckle extract into CTAB modified APT, the hydroxyl-related absorption peaks for HEA nanocomposites shifted to lower wavenumber, which suggested that there were hydrogen bonds between the oxygen-containing groups of organic molecules in honeysuckle extract and the hydrogen atoms of the alkane chain of CTAB modified APT [26]. This phenomenon also indicated that the honeysuckle extract was successfully bound on the surface of CTAB modified APT.

The crystalline structures of the relevant samples were analyzed by XRD over the 2θ range of 3° ~ 50°, and the XRD patterns of APT, CTAB modified APT and HEA nanocomposites were illustrated in Fig. 2b. There were obvious diffraction peaks at 2θ = 8.38°, 13.70°, 19.76°, 20.84°, 26.60°, and 34.73°, which belonged to the characteristic diffraction peaks of APT, corresponding to (110), (200), (040), (310), (231) and (440) crystal faces [6,27]. By contrast, the XRD patterns of CTAB modified APT and HEA nanocomposites also presented the same characteristic peaks of APT without the obvious changes in the position and intensity, indicating that the incorporation of CTAB and honeysuckle extract by the mechanical milling process had no significant effect on the structure of APT.

As shown in Fig. 2c and d, the mass loss for APT, CTAB modified APT and HEA was similar below 150 °C, which was attributed to the release of the physically absorbed water. The weight loss between 150 °C ~ 280 °C for APT was attributed to the loss of zeolite water, coordination water, while the mass loss of CTAB modified APT in the range of 150 °C–335 °C might be due to the degradation of CTAB molecules adsorbed on the APT surface [28]. For HEA, the weight loss was observed between 280 °C–350 °C was mainly due to the decomposition of organic matters [29]. The last weight loss for three samples ranging from 350 °C ~ 535 °C was mainly ascribed to the release of partial structural water, and the structural water was removed completely in the last stage (> 535 °C) [30]. By contrast, the successful incorporation of CTAB and honeysuckle extract on APT was clearly observed from the higher weight loss.

As depicted in Fig. 3 , APT presented a typical one-dimensional rod-like morphology. After being modified with CTAB, partial of nanorods were destroyed under the mechanical force. However, both of CTAB modified APT and HEA nanocomposites completely retained the nanorod-like morphology of APT, indicating that the rod-like crystal damage of APT was relatively small during the semi-dry grinding process. For honeysuckle extract powder, it was irregular-shaped particles with large sizes including flake-like and spherical morphologies with smooth surface, while the cavity structures were also observed for some spherical particles (Fig. S1). Furthermore, the vesicle structure with a diameter of 20–30 nm and NCDs derived from chlorogenic acid and other caffeoylquinic acid (yellow circle) were found from HRTEM after being extracted with ethanol (Fig. S2), and the diameter of NCDs was about 5–8 nm (Fig. S3). After introduction of honeysuckle extract, the surface of the rod crystals of APT became rough, suggesting that some honeysuckle extract was loaded on APT to form organic-inorganic nanocomposites, which were composed of C, O, N, Mg, Si and Al elements (Fig. S4). Meanwhile, the lattice fringes of NCDs were clearly observed at the edge of APT nanorods, and the interplanar spacing was 0.35 nm (Fig. S5), which indicated that NCDs presented a graphite-like structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physiochemical properties of HEAC composite films

ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of the as-prepared HEAC composite films was characterized by SEM (Fig. 4a and b). The pure CS films exhibited smooth and uniform surfaces and cross-sections. After incorporating HEA nanocomposites, the surface of the films gradually became rough. Within a certain range, HEA was evenly dispersed in the matrix without obvious agglomeration, which improved the mechanical strength of the films. This phenomenon might be related to the intermolecular hydrogen bonding and electrostatic attraction between CS molecules, APT and honeysuckle extract, thereby improving the surface/interface compatibility between the organic and inorganic components of composite films. However, the hydrogen bonds among APT rod crystals also caused the presence of the agglomeration in the matrix when the excessive HEA was added. Therefore, excessive HEA nanocomposites might have a negative impact on the relevant performances of composite films.

As shown in Fig. 4c, the FTIR spectra of HEAC composite films was similar to that of the pure CS films, while there was unnoticeable change in the functional groups, which suggested that the incorporated HEA did not change the chemical structure of CS. The peaks at 3253 cm−1 in CS film were attributed to OH and NH stretching vibrations [31], and the corresponding peaks shifted slightly to the low frequency region in HEAC composite films, suggesting that the formation of new hydrogen bond interaction between the polymer matrix and nanofillers [32]. For pure CS film, the peaks at 1642, 1558 and 1404 cm−1 were attributed to the CO stretching vibration (the amide bands I), NH deformation vibration (the amide bands II) and -CH3 symmetrical deformation, respectively [33]. Furthermore, the methylene stretching vibration of pyranose ring, the CO stretching vibration and the skeletal signals (vibration of glycosidic bonds) was observed at 2871 cm−1, 1026 cm−1 and between 1100–900 cm−1 [34]. These results indicated the infrared reflection spectra of HEAC composite films and CS film were almost similar, and the addition of HEA nanocomposites did not affect the peak position of CS in the FTIR spectrum.

The crystalline nature of the films was characterized with XRD (Fig. 4d), and the diffraction peak at 2θ = 20.10° was assigned to the (120) plane of the semi-crystalline structure of CS. In the case of HEAC-3, the characteristic diffraction peak of APT was observed at 2θ = 8.29° [30]. Meanwhile, a new diffraction peak appeared at 2θ = 11.15° and 17.80°, and the CS semi-crystalline diffraction peak became wider, indicating that the successful conjugation of CTAB modified APT and honeysuckle extract into CS [35]. Furthermore, TG and DTG analysis was used to investigate the thermal stability of HEAC composite films (Fig. 3e and f). Just as the literature reported, pure CS films mainly exhibited three weight losses: The first stage (< 100 °C) was attributed to the loss of physically absorbed water in the polymer, the second stage (100 °C ~ 231 °C) with a mass loss of 29.61 % was attributed to the removal of residual acetic acid and the decomposition of organic components, and the last one (231 °C ~ 410 °C) was due to the thermal degradation of the polymer chain [36]. After introduction of nanofillers, the TGA curve of HEAC composite films was similar to CS film, indicating that HEA nanofillers were uniformly dispersed in the CS matrix.

The water contact angle (WCA) can reflect the strength of the hydrophilicity and surface wettability of the films. As shown in Fig. 5a, CS film was hydrophobic with a WCA value of 100.26°, and the incorporation of HEA nanocomposites improved the WCA of CS film due to the well-dispersed nanorods produced roughness on the surface of composite film. By contrast, the WCA value increased with the increase in the added amount of HEA nanocomposites, and the changes leveled off as the added amount was above 4.76 %, which also indicated that the WCA value of biopolymer films was easily affected by nanofillers [37]. It suggested that the addition of HEA had a positive impact on the films to improve the hydrophobicity and enhance the durability of packaging materials.

The WC value of all films maintained in the range of 18–21 % (Fig. 5b) and the introduction of nanofillers had no significant effect on the WC of the films. It was reported that the cross-linking, porosity, fillers and material properties could affect the SD value of the films [5]. The SD results indicated that the SD value of composite films was higher than that of the pure CS films (Fig. 5c), suggesting that the introduction of nanofillers increased the porosity of the films and water molecules were easy to penetrate into the films. However, the excess nanofillers reduced the SD value of the films, because the hydrogen bonding interaction between the polymer and nanofillers increased network strength and density, thereby inhibiting water penetration into the films [38]. Thus, the WS value of the films decreased with the increasing added amount of HEA nanofillers (Fig. 5d).

Generally, the introduction of nanofillers played a crucial role on the mechanical properties of the polymer matrix [39]. From the Fig. 5e and f, it was observed that the TS and EB value of HEAC composite films were significantly higher than that of pure CS films (TS = 13.0 ± 0.79 MPa, EB = 38.6 ± 357 %), which was attributed to their dense structure formed by hydrogen bonds between CS, APT and honeysuckle extract, just as confirmed by FTIR analysis. The TS and EB value increased with the addition of HEA nanocomposite, and the TS and EB value reached the maximum of 24.9 ± 2.35 MPa and 64.8 ± 2.11 % after introduction of 4.76 % HEA nanocomposites, respectively. Afterwards, a downward trend appeared due to the presence of the agglomeration of excess nanofillers in the polymeric matrix and the unevenness of the matrix.

The films with excellent ultraviolet (UV) light blocking capabilities could effectively prevent the adverse effects of light toward quality changes [40]. The HEAC composites films was visually transparent as the CS films (Fig. 6a), and the transmittance values of all HEAC composite films in the wavelength range of 200–400 nm (UV region) were lower than the pure CS films (Fig. 6b), indicating that the HEAC composite films had a certain ability to block UV light. This result might be related to the presence of NCDs, various organic acids and flavonoids in honeysuckle extract, and the abundant unsaturated bonds could absorb UV light. The transmittance of pure CS films reached 84.2 % in the wavelength range > 400 nm, and the light transmittance of the films decreased slightly after incorporation of HEA nanocomposites, which was attributed to light scattering absorption or reflection at the interface of the CS matrix and nanofillers [41].

In addition, all as-prepared films exhibited high L
⁎ value, indicating high transparency (Fig. 6c and d). Compared with pure CS films, the addition of HEA nanocomposites resulted in a slight decrease in brightness and an increase in redness of HEAC composite films. As the b
⁎ value increased, the yellowness of HEAC composite film increased. Meanwhile, the total color change of the films could be reflected by the ΔE
⁎ value before and after incorporation of nanofillers. With increase in the addition amount of HEA nanocomposite, the ΔE
⁎ value of composite films also increased. Although there are certain differences in the surface color of composite films, it did not affect the appearance of the involved food.

As shown in Fig. 6e, the excitation wavelength of HEAC composite films was 360 nm, while the center of the peak in the emission spectrum was at 466 nm, which was basically consistent with the excitation spectrum of NCDs derived from chlorogenic acid with a minor difference [17]. The change might be related to other components of the honeysuckle extract (e.g., other mono-caffeoylquinic acid). It was reported that dozens of compounds were identified from honeysuckle extract, including a variety of mono-caffeoylquinic acid and dicaffeoylquinic acid [16]. Due to the existence of multiple isomers, the excitation spectrum of the films was not completely consistent with that of chlorogenic acid-based NCDs. Furthermore, HEAC composite films presented strong blue fluorescence after being exposed to UV light irradiation (Fig. 6f), which was consistent with that of emission spectrum. The fluorescence emission spectra of HEAC composite films with different added amount of HEA nanocomposite was recorded in Fig. 6g, and the fluorescence intensity of HEAC composite films gradually increased with the increase in the added amount of HEA nanocomposite. Meanwhile, the effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of HEAC composite film (Fig. 6h), and the maximum fluorescence intensity occurred at pH = 3, and then the fluorescence intensity of HEAC composite films decreased with the increase in the pH value. The change in the intensity might be related to the deprotonation of carboxyl groups in the surface of caffeoquinic acid-based NCDs in honeysuckle extract (e.g., chlorogenic acid), which disrupted the surface charge and its emissivity [42]. The process of food spoilage will cause pH changes [19], and thus HEAC composite films presented the potential for intelligent detection with the release of pH-sensitive substance due to the food spoilage.

Fig. 7 showed the antibacterial activity of CS films and HEAC composite films against E. coli and S. aureus. Obviously, the CS films presented a certain antibacterial activity by impairing the integrity of the outer membrane and cell wall [43], and the antibacterial effect was significantly improved after incorporation of HEA nanocomposites, and the maximum antibacterial ratio against E. coli and S. aureus reached 99.27 ± 0.18 % and 98.85 ± 0.65 % respectively. The excellent antibacterial activity of HEAC composite films were mainly attributed to the synergistic effect of the following factors. First, APT nanorods could enhance the interfacial interaction between the antibacterial components loaded on APT and bacteria. Furthermore, the one-dimensional nanorods of APT served as “elongate needles”, and the HEA nanocomposite loaded with honeysuckle extract was like a “poison needle” [44]. As APT pierced the peptidoglycan layer of the bacterial cells, NCDs and active ingredients from honeysuckle extract were released into bacteria, thereby destroying DNA, proteins and enzymes inside the bacteria to successfully kill the bacteria. The last but not the least, honeysuckle extract contained a variety of effective active substances, such as organic acids, flavonoids and triterpene saponins, which exhibited good inhibitory effects on the bacteria [29].

In addition, the singlet oxygen could induce apoptosis, cell necrosis and autophagy [45], and DPBF reacted via 1,3-addition mechanism with 1O2, which could be applied in the indirect detection of the ROS form [46]. Fig. 7c presented the example set of UV–vis spectra of DPBF solution in DMSO recorded during illumination of HEAC-3 composite films with 365 nm laser. Under the irradiation of 365 nm light source, HEAC-3 composite films decreased the absorbance of DPBF, which indicated that NCDs in honeysuckle extract could be activated by ultraviolet irradiation to produce singlet oxygen and exert antibacterial effects.

Oxidative damage is a key factor inducing food spoilage. Honeysuckle extract exhibited excellent antibacterial properties and good antioxidant capacity. Therefore, the ·OH and DPPH radical scavenging activities of CS films and HEAC composite films were evaluated (Fig. 8 a and b). The ·OH radical scavenging efficiency of HEAC-4 composite films reached 95.46 %, which was twice that of the pure CS films (40.96 %). The possible mechanism for the antioxidant activity of CS films was the formation of stable macromolecular radicals and ammonium groups between the residual free amino groups of CS and DPPH or hydroxyl radicals [47]. Therefore, the improvement of the antioxidant capacity of HEAC composite films was mainly attributed to the alcohols and esters in honeysuckle extract. Some studies also confirmed that chlorogenic acid-based NCDs also possessed a certain antioxidant activity [17]. Furthermore, the DPPH free radical scavenging efficiency of HEAC-4 composite films reached 56.62 %, which was nearly doubled compared with CS films (37.47 %). These results suggested that introduction of HEA nanocomposites into composite films could effectively improve the antioxidant activity of CS films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: HEAC composite films for fruit preservation
ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by the excellent antibacterial and antioxidant properties of the obtained composite films, the potential application of HEAC composite films in food preservation were further explored. The visual observation of control and coated strawberries throughout the storage days was depicted in Fig. 8c. All strawberries were fresh after being stored for 1 day, and then the control group and CS-coated strawberries began to decay and turn black on the second day, while the rest of the strawberries coated with film precursor solution were fresh. However, the decay was also observed for HEAC-1-coated strawberries on the third day, and neither decay nor shrinkage occurred for HEAC-2, HEAC-3 and HEAC-4 coated strawberries, which could be attributed to the good antibacterial and antioxidant properties of HEA nanocomposites. Generally, the fruit decay is closely related to the storage temperature. The higher temperature, the more difficulty to store. Compared with the reported ones, the prepared HEAC also presented better preservation effect at 25 °C, and the shelf life of strawberries was effectively extended. The thickness of the coatings on the strawberry surface was 0.076 ± 0.009 mm measured by optical microscope (Fig. S6), which was almost consistent with the thickness of 0.06 mm for the prepared composite films by casting and solvent evaporation (Table S2).

Furthermore, the strawberries packaged with composite films also exhibited a better preservation effect than the control group and pure chitosan film (Fig. S7). Both of the strawberries in the control group and pure chitosan group rotted and molded after being stored for 4 days, while the quality of the strawberries packaged with HEAC composite films was obviously better than that of the control group and pure chitosan group. It suggested that the prepared material exhibited good preservation effect on the strawberries either the coatings or the films. Thus, HEAC composite films presented good antioxidant, antibacterial activity and moisture retention ability to extend the storage period of the fruits (Table 1).

In the study, CS-based composite films with fluorescence detection property were successfully prepared after incorporation of functional nanofillers composed of natural APT and honeysuckle extract containing natural carbon dots for intelligent food packaging. Due to the synergistic effect of multi-components in composite films, the obtained composite films presented good thermal stability, UV-blocking, mechanical, antibacterial and antioxidant properties, and the optimal antibacterial ratios of composite films against E. coli and S. aureus were 99.27 ± 0.18 % and 98.85, respectively. Furthermore, the composite films also presented the application potential for detecting food spoilage based on the change of fluorescence intensity with the change of the pH value. Thus, this study provided a promising strategy for construction of intelligent food packaging combining with natural clay mineral and plant extract containing carbon dots. However, the formation mechanism of natural carbon dots in honeysuckle extract should be continuously explored to guide the design of high-performance intelligent biodegradable food packaging materials in the future.
